question_text: A mobile phone transmits data using microwaves with a frequency of 2.4 × 10⁹ Hz. Radio waves used for FM broadcasting have a frequency of 1.0 × 10⁸ Hz. Calculate the ratio of the wavelength of the FM radio wave to the wavelength of the microwave, and explain why this difference matters for antenna design.
correct_answer: The ratio is 24:1 (or 24). The FM radio wave has a wavelength 24 times larger than the microwave. This matters because antennas must be similar in size to the wavelength they receive, so FM radio antennas are much longer than mobile phone antennas.
explanation: Using the wave equation v = fλ, and since all EM waves travel at the same speed (3 × 10⁸ m/s), the wavelength is inversely proportional to frequency. The ratio of wavelengths equals the inverse ratio of frequencies: (1.0 × 10⁸)/(2.4 × 10⁹) = 1/24, so the FM wavelength is 24 times larger. Antenna size is crucial for efficient signal reception, which is why mobile antennas are tiny while FM radio antennas are much longer.
